sectionName: Chemical and Physical Foundations of Biological Systems
topicArea: Acid-Base Chemistry and Buffers / Colligative Properties

questions.passage:
A chemistry student titrated 50.0 mL of 0.10 M acetic acid (CH₃COOH, Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵, pKa = 4.74) with 0.10 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH). The pH was recorded at several volumes of NaOH added.

Volume NaOH added (mL) | pH
0 | 2.87
10 | 4.14
25 | 4.74
50 | 8.72
60 | 12.0

The initial solution contained 5.0 mmol of acetic acid. At each point before the equivalence point, a buffer solution of acetic acid and sodium acetate was present. At the equivalence point (50 mL NaOH), all acetic acid had been converted to sodium acetate (CH₃COO⁻Na⁺). Beyond the equivalence point, excess NaOH determined the pH.

Figure 1 shows the titration curve generated from these pH measurements.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Titration curve for 50.0 mL of 0.10 M acetic acid titrated with 0.10 M NaOH. The half-equivalence point occurs at 25 mL and the equivalence point at 50 mL.
questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, at the half-equivalence point (25 mL NaOH added), the pH equals the pKa of acetic acid (4.74). This relationship holds because:

choices.A: all the acetic acid has been neutralized, leaving only acetate ion in solution
choices.B: the concentrations of acetic acid and acetate ion are equal, causing the log term in the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to equal zero
choices.C: the solution has lost all buffering capacity at this point
choices.D: the strong base has reacted with water to produce a neutral solution

explanations.A: At the half-equivalence point, only half of the acetic acid has been neutralized. Of the original 5.0 mmol of acetic acid, 2.5 mmol have reacted with 2.5 mmol NaOH to produce 2.5 mmol acetate, while 2.5 mmol of acetic acid remain. Complete neutralization occurs at the equivalence point (50 mL), not at 25 mL.
explanations.B: At the half-equivalence point, exactly half the acid has been converted to its conjugate base: [CH₃COOH] = [CH₃COO⁻] = 2.5 mmol. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). When [A⁻] = [HA], the ratio is 1 and log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa. This is the defining feature of the half-equivalence point and the point at which the buffer has maximum capacity to resist pH changes in either direction.
explanations.C: The half-equivalence point is actually the point of maximum buffer capacity, not the loss of it. A buffer is most effective when [HA] ≈ [A⁻], which is exactly the condition at the half-equivalence point. Buffer capacity is lost near the equivalence point, where nearly all the weak acid has been consumed.
explanations.D: NaOH is a strong base that reacts with the weak acid, not with water, in this titration. The pH of 4.74 is acidic, not neutral, and reflects the equilibrium properties of the acetic acid/acetate buffer at equal concentrations.

questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, the pH at the equivalence point (8.72) is above 7.0 because:

choices.A: excess NaOH remains in solution after neutralization is complete
choices.B: the Na⁺ ion from NaOH hydrolyzes water to produce hydroxide ions
choices.C: residual acetic acid donates protons to maintain the pH above neutral
choices.D: the acetate ion, as the conjugate base of the weak acid, hydrolyzes water to produce OH⁻, shifting the equilibrium toward a basic pH

explanations.A: At the equivalence point, stoichiometrically equal moles of acid and base have reacted, so no excess NaOH remains. The excess NaOH scenario applies beyond the equivalence point, such as at 60 mL where pH = 12.0. The basic pH at the equivalence point arises from the properties of the product, sodium acetate, not from unreacted NaOH.
explanations.B: Na⁺ is the conjugate acid of the strong base NaOH and does not undergo hydrolysis in water. Alkali metal cations (Li⁺, Na⁺, K⁺) are spectator ions with negligible Lewis acidity in aqueous solution. The basic pH at the equivalence point is due to acetate ion hydrolysis, not Na⁺ hydrolysis.
explanations.C: At the equivalence point, all acetic acid has been converted to acetate. There is no significant residual acetic acid to donate protons. The pH of 8.72 is basic, which is inconsistent with proton donation by a residual acid.
explanations.D: At the equivalence point, the solution contains 5.0 mmol of sodium acetate in 100 mL (0.050 M). The acetate ion is the conjugate base of the weak acid acetic acid and undergoes hydrolysis: CH₃COO⁻ + H₂O ⇌ CH₃COOH + OH⁻. The equilibrium constant for this reaction is Kb = Kw/Ka = 10⁻¹⁴/(1.8 × 10⁻⁵) = 5.6 × 10⁻¹⁰. The OH⁻ produced raises the pH above 7.0 to 8.72. This is why the equivalence point of a weak acid-strong base titration is always above 7.

questions.topic: Equivalence point pH in weak acid-strong base titrations

questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, the acetic acid/acetate buffer system has its maximum buffering capacity at:

choices.A: the half-equivalence point (25 mL NaOH), where [CH₃COOH] = [CH₃COO⁻] and the buffer can equally absorb added acid or base
choices.B: the equivalence point (50 mL NaOH), where the maximum amount of conjugate base is present
choices.C: the initial point (0 mL NaOH), where the acid concentration is at its highest
choices.D: 60 mL NaOH, where excess strong base provides additional OH⁻ to absorb added acid

explanations.A: Buffer capacity is the ability of a buffer to resist pH changes upon addition of acid or base. Maximum buffer capacity occurs when [HA] = [A⁻], which is at the half-equivalence point. At this point, the buffer contains equal reservoirs of acid (to neutralize added base) and conjugate base (to neutralize added acid), giving it the greatest capacity to absorb perturbations in either direction. This corresponds to the region of the titration curve with the smallest slope (pH changes least per mL of NaOH added).
explanations.B: At the equivalence point, all the weak acid has been converted to conjugate base, and there is no reservoir of weak acid remaining. This means the solution has essentially no capacity to absorb added base, and its ability to absorb added acid is limited to the reverse hydrolysis reaction. The buffer has been exhausted at this point.
explanations.C: At the initial point, there is maximum acid but minimal conjugate base (only the small amount produced by autoionization). A buffer requires both components to function effectively. With virtually no acetate present, the solution cannot absorb added acid because there is no conjugate base to react with H⁺.
explanations.D: Beyond the equivalence point, the solution is no longer a buffer system. The pH is determined by the concentration of excess NaOH, and the solution behaves as a strong base solution. While excess OH⁻ can neutralize added acid, this is not buffering in the technical sense.

questions.stem: If the student repeated the titration using 0.10 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) instead of acetic acid, the equivalence point pH would be:

choices.A: above 7.0, because Cl⁻ hydrolyzes water to produce hydroxide ions
choices.B: above 8.72, because HCl is a stronger acid and produces a more basic salt upon neutralization
choices.C: exactly 7.0, because the reaction of a strong acid with a strong base yields water and a neutral salt that does not hydrolyze
choices.D: below 7.0, because the Cl⁻ ion is an acidic anion that donates protons to water

explanations.A: Cl⁻ is the conjugate base of the strong acid HCl and is an extremely weak base—too weak to undergo any measurable hydrolysis. It is a spectator ion in aqueous solution. Only conjugate bases of weak acids (such as acetate, fluoride, or carbonate) hydrolyze water to produce basic solutions.
explanations.B: The strength of the acid does not determine the basicity of the equivalence point in a strong acid-strong base titration. The equivalence point of a strong acid-strong base titration is always pH 7.0 at 25°C because the product (NaCl + H₂O) does not hydrolyze. The basic equivalence point of the acetic acid titration (8.72) arose specifically because acetic acid is a weak acid whose conjugate base hydrolyzes.
explanations.C: The reaction HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O produces sodium chloride and water. Neither Na⁺ nor Cl⁻ undergoes hydrolysis: Na⁺ is the cation of a strong base, and Cl⁻ is the anion of a strong acid. The resulting solution is neutral at pH 7.0. This contrasts with the weak acid titration, where the conjugate base (acetate) hydrolyzes to produce a basic equivalence point.
explanations.D: Cl⁻ does not donate protons to water. As the conjugate base of a strong acid (HCl, which fully dissociates), Cl⁻ has no tendency to accept protons from water and therefore does not affect the pH. It is a spectator ion that makes no contribution to the acid-base equilibrium.

questions.stem: Arterial blood is maintained near pH 7.40 by the bicarbonate buffer system (HCO₃⁻/CO₂, pKa = 6.10). Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, the [HCO₃⁻] to [CO₂] ratio at pH 7.40 is closest to:

explanations.A: A 1:1 ratio of conjugate base to acid would give pH = pKa = 6.10, which is far below the physiological pH of 7.40. The blood pH is 1.30 units above the pKa, indicating that the conjugate base (HCO₃⁻) substantially exceeds the acid (dissolved CO₂).
explanations.B: Applying the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([HCO₃⁻]/[CO₂]). Substituting: 7.40 = 6.10 + log(ratio), so log(ratio) = 1.30. Therefore ratio = 10^1.30 ≈ 20. The blood maintains approximately 24 mM HCO₃⁻ and 1.2 mM dissolved CO₂, giving a ratio of 20:1. This large excess of base over acid means the system is more effective at buffering added acid than added base, which is physiologically appropriate since metabolic processes generate more acid than base.
explanations.C: A 2:1 ratio would give log(2) = 0.30, yielding pH = 6.10 + 0.30 = 6.40. This is far below the arterial pH of 7.40 and would represent a severe, life-threatening acidosis.
explanations.D: A 0.05:1 ratio means [HCO₃⁻] is 20 times less than [CO₂], giving log(0.05) = −1.30 and pH = 6.10 − 1.30 = 4.80. This is inverted from the correct ratio and would represent a pH incompatible with life.

questions.passage:
A biochemistry student studied colligative properties of aqueous solutions to compare the behavior of electrolyte and nonelectrolyte solutes and to determine the molecular weight of an unknown protein.

In Experiment 1, 10.0 g of NaCl (MW = 58.44 g/mol) was dissolved in 1.000 kg of water, and the freezing point depression was measured.

In Experiment 2, 5.00 g of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, MW = 180.16 g/mol) was dissolved in 1.000 kg of water, and the freezing point depression was measured.

In Experiment 3, 10.0 g of an unknown protein was dissolved in 1.000 L of water at 25°C, and the osmotic pressure was measured as 0.0041 atm.

Relevant constants:
Kf (water) = 1.86 °C·kg/mol
R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)
Normal freezing point of water = 0.00°C
questions.stem: The freezing point depression of the NaCl solution in Experiment 1 is closest to:

explanations.A: This value would result from using a van't Hoff factor of 1 (no dissociation) and a molality of approximately 0.10 m: ΔTf = 1 × 1.86 × 0.10 = 0.186°C. NaCl is a strong electrolyte that fully dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻, so the van't Hoff factor must be approximately 2, and the correct molality is 0.171 m.
explanations.B: This value results from using i = 1 with the correct molality: ΔTf = 1 × 1.86 × 0.171 = 0.318°C. This calculation neglects the dissociation of NaCl into two ions. Because colligative properties depend on the total number of solute particles, the van't Hoff factor must account for the ions produced.
explanations.C: This value does not correspond to any standard calculation error. The correct calculation requires moles NaCl = 10.0/58.44 = 0.171 mol, molality = 0.171 m, i = 2, giving ΔTf = 2 × 1.86 × 0.171 = 0.636°C ≈ 0.64°C.
explanations.D: Moles of NaCl = 10.0 g / 58.44 g/mol = 0.171 mol. Molality = 0.171 mol / 1.000 kg = 0.171 m. NaCl dissociates completely: NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻, so the van't Hoff factor i = 2. Applying ΔTf = i × Kf × m = 2 × 1.86 × 0.171 = 0.636°C ≈ 0.64°C. The new freezing point is approximately −0.64°C.

questions.stem: Using the osmotic pressure data from Experiment 3, the molecular weight of the unknown protein is closest to:

explanations.A: Using the osmotic pressure equation π = MRT, where M is molarity: M = π/(RT) = 0.0041/(0.0821 × 298) = 0.0041/24.47 = 1.68 × 10⁻⁴ mol/L. In 1.000 L of solution, this represents 1.68 × 10⁻⁴ mol of protein. The molecular weight is MW = mass/moles = 10.0 g / 1.68 × 10⁻⁴ mol = 59,500 g/mol ≈ 60,000 g/mol. This is a reasonable molecular weight for a medium-sized protein.
explanations.B: This value is tenfold too small and would arise from a calculation error such as using 1.0 g instead of 10.0 g, or from an arithmetic error in the molarity calculation. A protein with MW of 6,000 g/mol would produce a tenfold higher osmotic pressure at the same mass concentration.
explanations.C: A molecular weight of 600 g/mol is characteristic of a small molecule, not a protein. At 10.0 g in 1.0 L, this would produce a molarity of 0.0167 M, which would generate an osmotic pressure approximately 100 times larger than the measured 0.0041 atm.
explanations.D: This value is tenfold too large and would arise from a calculation error such as multiplying instead of dividing by a factor of 10. A protein with MW = 600,000 g/mol would produce an osmotic pressure only one-tenth as large as observed, approximately 0.0004 atm.

questions.stem: Compared to NaCl, glucose produces a much smaller freezing point depression per gram of solute because:

choices.A: glucose forms hydrogen bonds with water that stabilize the liquid phase and resist freezing
choices.B: glucose has a lower van't Hoff factor because it is a stronger electrolyte than NaCl
choices.C: glucose has a higher molecular weight (180.16 g/mol vs. 58.44 g/mol) and does not dissociate in solution, resulting in fewer solute particles per gram
choices.D: glucose is less soluble in water than NaCl, so a smaller fraction actually dissolves

explanations.A: While glucose does form hydrogen bonds with water, this is not the primary reason for the smaller freezing point depression per gram. Colligative properties depend on the number of solute particles, not on solute-solvent interactions. NaCl also interacts strongly with water through ion-dipole forces. The key factor is particle count per gram.
explanations.B: Glucose is a nonelectrolyte with a van't Hoff factor of 1 (no dissociation), while NaCl is a strong electrolyte with i = 2. Glucose has a lower van't Hoff factor because it does not dissociate, not because it is a stronger electrolyte. The statement reverses the relationship: NaCl dissociates more, not less, than glucose.
explanations.C: Colligative properties depend on the total number of dissolved solute particles per kilogram of solvent. Per gram, glucose produces fewer particles than NaCl for two reasons: its higher molecular weight means fewer moles per gram (5.55 mmol/g for glucose vs. 17.1 mmol/g for NaCl), and its van't Hoff factor is 1 versus 2 for NaCl. Together, 1 g of NaCl produces approximately 6 times as many particles as 1 g of glucose, resulting in approximately 6 times the freezing point depression per gram.
explanations.D: Both NaCl and glucose are freely soluble in water at the concentrations used in these experiments. Solubility is not the limiting factor. At 5.00 g per kg, glucose is well below its saturation point. The difference in freezing point depression is due to the number of particles produced per gram, not solubility limitations.

questions.stem: If the protein in Experiment 3 reversibly dimerizes in solution (two monomers associating into one dimer), the measured osmotic pressure would be:

choices.A: unchanged, because osmotic pressure depends on total solute mass rather than particle number
choices.B: higher than expected for the monomer, because the larger dimer occupies more solution volume
choices.C: identical to the monomeric prediction, because dimerization does not affect colligative properties
choices.D: lower than predicted for the monomeric form, because dimerization reduces the effective number of solute particles in solution

explanations.A: Osmotic pressure, like all colligative properties, depends on the number of solute particles (moles), not on their total mass. Two monomers combining into one dimer reduces the particle count by one while the total mass remains constant. This reduction in particle number produces a measurable decrease in osmotic pressure.
explanations.B: The volume occupied by the solute is negligible in dilute solution and does not significantly affect osmotic pressure. The larger size of the dimer does not create an additional osmotic effect. Osmotic pressure is determined by the molar concentration of solute particles according to π = MRT, where M is molarity.
explanations.C: Dimerization directly affects colligative properties by reducing the number of independent solute particles. If all monomers dimerize, the molar concentration is halved, and osmotic pressure drops by approximately half. Colligative properties are sensitive to any process that changes particle number, including association (dimerization) and dissociation (electrolyte ionization).
explanations.D: When two protein monomers associate to form one dimer, the total number of solute particles decreases. If N monomers fully dimerize, the solution contains N/2 particles instead of N, halving the effective molar concentration. Since π = MRT, the osmotic pressure decreases proportionally. This effect would cause the calculated molecular weight to appear higher than the true monomer weight, because fewer particles at the same mass implies a higher mass per particle.

questions.stem: Osmotic pressure rather than freezing point depression was used to determine the protein's molecular weight because:

choices.A: osmotic pressure is proportional to molarity and produces measurable values even at the very low molar concentrations characteristic of dissolved macromolecules, whereas freezing point depression at such concentrations would be too small to detect accurately
choices.B: freezing point depression requires electrolyte solutes and cannot be applied to proteins
choices.C: osmotic pressure measurements are independent of temperature, making them more accurate under all laboratory conditions
choices.D: freezing point depression depends on solute molecular weight rather than particle number, making it unsuitable for molecular weight determination

explanations.A: Macromolecules such as proteins have very high molecular weights, meaning that even gram quantities produce very low molar concentrations. For a 60,000 g/mol protein at 10 g/L, the molarity is only ~1.7 × 10⁻⁴ M. The corresponding freezing point depression would be ΔTf = 1.86 × 1.7 × 10⁻⁴ = 0.00032°C, which is far too small to measure accurately with standard equipment. Osmotic pressure, however, yields π = MRT = 1.7 × 10⁻⁴ × 0.0821 × 298 = 0.0041 atm, which can be measured with a simple membrane osmometer. This practical advantage makes osmometry the preferred method for macromolecular weight determination.
explanations.B: Freezing point depression applies to all solutes, both electrolyte and nonelectrolyte, including proteins. The limitation is not the type of solute but the magnitude of the effect: proteins produce extremely small freezing point depressions because of their low molar concentrations, making measurement impractical.
explanations.C: Osmotic pressure depends on temperature (π = MRT), so it is not temperature-independent. Its advantage over freezing point depression for macromolecular characterization is the larger magnitude of the measured signal at low molar concentrations, not temperature independence.
explanations.D: Freezing point depression, like all colligative properties, depends on the number of solute particles (molality), not on molecular weight directly. It can in principle be used for molecular weight determination (by measuring ΔTf and calculating molality, then deriving MW from mass and moles). The practical problem is that the ΔTf produced by high-molecular-weight solutes is too small to measure accurately.

questions.topic: Practical considerations in colligative property measurements
